perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall limit disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its directors, officers, partners, members and/or employees having a need to know Confidential Information and shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party (whether an individual, corporation, or other entity) without the prior written consent of the other party, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld. The Parties shall satisfy their obligations under this paragraph if they take affirmative measures to ensure compliance with these confidentiality obligations by its employees, agents, consultants and others who are permitted access to or use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall limit disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its directors, officers, partners, members and/or employees having a need to know Confidential Information and shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party (whether an individual, corporation, or other entity) without the prior written consent of the other party, which consent may be withheld at the sole discretion of the disclosing party. The Parties shall satisfy their obligations under this paragraph if they take affirmative measures to ensure compliance with these confidentiality obligations by its agents and consultants who are permitted access to or use of the Confidential Information, but not necessarily its employees.
perturbation.explanation: This change makes the consent to disclose to third parties subject to the sole discretion of the disclosing party, which could be deemed an unreasonable restriction on the use of confidential information. Additionally, it removes the explicit requirement for affirmative measures to ensure compliance by employees, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use without consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. The modified text weakens the obligation to ensure employees comply with confidentiality, potentially conflicting with the reasonable efforts standard required to maintain trade secret protection under CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets by removing the explicit requirement for affirmative measures to ensure compliance by employees. The original text states, "The Parties shall satisfy their obligations under this paragraph if they take affirmative measures to ensure compliance with these confidentiality obligations by its employees, agents, consultants and others who are permitted access to or use of the Confidential Information." The changed text removes "employees" from this clause. Snippet 1 defines misappropriation to include unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret, especially when the knowledge was acquired under circumstances creating a duty to maintain secrecy. By removing the explicit requirement for affirmative measures regarding employees, the changed text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure by employees, potentially violating the duty to maintain secrecy required to protect trade secrets under CIV 3426.1(b)(2).

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement imposes no obligation upon the Parties with respect to any Confidential Information (a) that was possessed by the receiving party before receipt; (b) is, or becomes a matter of, public knowledge through no fault of the receiving party; (c) is rightfully received from a third party not owing a duty of confidentiality to the disclosing party; (d) is disclosed without a duty of confidentiality to a third party by, or with the authorization of, the disclosing party; or (e) is independently developed.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement imposes no obligation upon the Parties with respect to any Confidential Information (a) that was possessed by the receiving party before receipt; (b) becomes public knowledge, regardless of the receiving party's fault; (c) is received from a third party, regardless of any duty of confidentiality; (d) is disclosed to a third party by the disclosing party; or (e) is independently developed.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the condition that public knowledge must occur through no fault of the receiving party, and that a third party must not owe a duty of confidentiality. This significantly weakens the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, information is protected as a trade secret if it derives independent economic value from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. By removing the 'no fault' clause and the requirement for the third party to not have a duty of confidentiality, the agreement potentially allows for the disclosure of information that would otherwise be protected as a trade secret, directly contradicting CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes key protections for confidential information, contradicting the spirit of trade secret law. Specifically, the original text required that public knowledge of confidential information occur 'through no fault of the receiving party' to lose its protected status, and that third parties receiving the information do not owe a duty of confidentiality. The modified text removes these conditions, meaning that even if the receiving party's actions caused the information to become public, or if a third party with a duty of confidentiality discloses it, the information would no longer be protected. This contradicts the intention to protect trade secrets as described in CIV 3426.1, where 'improper means' include 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By removing the "no fault" and "duty of confidentiality" clauses, the agreement weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially allowing for their misappropriation via improper means without recourse.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any dispute involving this Agreement, the parties hereby agree that such dispute shall be resolved in the appropriate State or Federal court, in the county of Los Angeles, state of California, and the parties hereby agree that proper venue and jurisdiction shall lie with any court of competent jurisdiction in the county of Los Angeles, state of California.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any dispute involving this Agreement, the parties hereby agree that such dispute shall be resolved in a court of the disclosing party's choosing, regardless of jurisdiction or venue. The parties agree that proper venue and jurisdiction shall lie with the court selected by the disclosing party, regardless of where the confidential information originated or where the receiving party is located.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the disclosing party to choose any court, regardless of whether it has jurisdiction or proper venue. This is highly unusual and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 395 dictates the proper venue for civil actions, generally based on the defendant's residence or where the obligation was to be performed. The modified text contradicts this by giving the disclosing party the sole discretion to choose any venue, regardless of these statutory requirements, potentially depriving the receiving party of their right to a fair trial in a convenient location.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. OF THE PLACE OF TRIAL, RECLASSIFICATION, AND COORDINATION OF CIVIL ACTIONS [392 - 404.9] ( Heading of Title 4 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. Place of Trial [392 - 403] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 7. ) 395. (a) Except as otherwise provided by law and subject to the power of the court to transfer actions or proceedings as provided in this title, the superior court in the county where the defendants or some of them reside at the commencement of the action is the proper court for the trial of the action. If the action is for injury to person or personal property or for death from wrongful act or negligence, the superior court in either the county where the injury occurs or the injury causing death occurs or the county where the defendants, or some of them reside at the commencement of the action, is a proper court for the trial of the action. In a proceeding for dissolution of marriage, the superior court in the county where either the petitioner or respondent has been a resident for three months next preceding the commencement of the proceeding is the proper court for the trial of the proceeding. In a proceeding for nulli
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 395 by allowing the disclosing party to choose any court regardless of jurisdiction or venue, thereby undermining the statutory requirements for proper venue as dictated by CCP 395(a). CCP 395(a) states that "the superior court in the county where the defendants or some of them reside at the commencement of the action is the proper court for the trial of the action," unless otherwise provided by law. The changed text removes the receiving party's right to a trial in a convenient location, creating a significant departure from legally established venue guidelines.

perturbation.original_text: All notices hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given if delivered personally, one day after delivery to a nationally recognized overnight delivery service, charges prepaid, or three days after being sent by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, to the parties at their respective addresses set forth above and:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices hereunder may be given orally, and shall be deemed to have been duly given at the moment of utterance, regardless of whether the receiving party actually hears or understands the notice. Written notice is optional and at the discretion of the notifying party.
perturbation.explanation: This drastically alters the notice requirements, making them extremely informal and unreliable. It removes the requirement for written notice and allows for oral notice, even if not received.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section outlines the essentials of consent in contract law, many contractual obligations and legal proceedings require 'clear and unambiguous' notice to ensure due process and fairness. The change to allow for oral notice that might not even be heard contradicts this principle. While section 1565 does not explicitly state how notice is to be communicated, case law and common legal practices generally require that legal notices are formal and documented, which the change undermines. Oral notice is problematic in establishing verifiable proof of delivery and receipt, potentially rendering any provision reliant on notice unenforceable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Consent [1565 - 1590] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1565. The consent of the parties to a contract must be: 1. Free; 2. Mutual; and, 3. Communicated by each to the other. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates written notice, while the changed text allows oral notice, even if not received. This directly contradicts the principle of 'communicated by each to the other' as outlined in California Civil Code Section 1565. While the code does not explicitly state that communication must be written, the shift to allowing potentially unreceived oral notice undermines the core requirement of mutual communication, thereby creating a legal contradiction because it makes verifiable proof of delivery and receipt difficult.
